source: Hand-built from fixtures/evals/cases/thesis-2026-07-01.json (2 findings + 2 standing theses, verbatim) + a synthesized scorecard summary; recordedAnswer is a clean two-line implication answer citing those ids.

findings.statement: TSMC reported 100% capacity utilization for its 3nm node in H1 2026, with demand roughly three times available supply.
findings.why: Leading-edge foundry capacity is the binding upstream constraint on both GPU and custom-ASIC output.

impact.mechanism: Full 3nm utilization with 3x oversubscription caps shippable advanced-chip volume.

evidence.excerpt: TSMC reported 100% capacity utilization for its 3nm node in H1 2026, with demand running approximately three times higher than available supply.

findings.statement: NVIDIA Data Center revenue was a record $75.2 billion in Q1 FY2027, up 92% year over year and 21% sequentially.
findings.why: Record Data Center revenue growth quantifies the scale and pace of AI infrastructure demand.

impact.mechanism: Blackwell-led data-center sales drive merchant-GPU revenue and reflect hyperscale/neocloud buildout.

evidence.excerpt: First-quarter revenue was a record $75.2 billion, up 21% from the previous quarter and up 92% from a year ago.

bottleneck.rationale: Foundry and packaging capacity is the binding cap on shippable supply.

momentum.rationale: Record datacenter revenue shows demand outrunning supply.

scorecard.narrative: Demand outruns a supply ceiling set by advanced packaging and leading-edge foundry capacity.

categoryStatus.bottleneck: advanced packaging and leading-edge foundry capacity
categoryStatus.reason: Demand momentum outruns the supply ramp.

entries.title: NVDA demand durability
entries.statement: Datacenter GPU demand continues to outrun NVIDIA's ability to serve it.
entries.mechanism: Booked orders, financed deployments and multi-GW campus commitments accumulate faster than NVIDIA can ship, sustaining a demand overhang.
entries.falsifiableTrigger: Backlog/RPO growth falls below shipment growth for 2 consecutive quarters.
entries.sensitivity: Hyperscaler and AI-cloud capex plans and the pace of Vera Rubin supply ramp.

entries.title: Supply constraint binding
entries.statement: Advanced packaging + HBM — not wafers or demand — cap realizable merchant-GPU revenue.
entries.mechanism: Advanced-packaging (CoWoS) and HBM capacity gate how much wafer and demand can be converted into shippable accelerators, so realizable revenue tracks packaging/HBM output.
entries.falsifiableTrigger: CoWoS/HBM leadTimes normalize to historical norms for 2 consecutive quarters while demand indicators stay positive.
entries.sensitivity: Packaging (Amkor/TSMC) and HBM capacity additions relative to the Vera Rubin ramp.

recordedAnswer: {"lines": [{"watchItem": "Advanced-packaging and leading-edge foundry tightness stays the binding cap on how much demand converts to shippable supply, holding the revenue ceiling below end-market pull.", "dimensions": ["bottleneck"], "thesisIds": ["supply-constraint-binding"], "findingIds": ["introl-com-ef18d500-4"]}, {"watchItem": "Datacenter accelerator demand keeps outrunning available supply, so foundry pull stays firm as long as that gap holds.", "dimensions": ["momentum"], "thesisIds": ["nvda-demand-durability"], "findingIds": ["nvidianews-nvidia-com-7b7a02ff-1"]}]}
notes: Positive implication case: watch-items/exposure statements (never recommendations), each citing a scorecard dimension + standing thesis + finding that resolves. Good looks like sharp exposure framing a TSMC exec can act on; no should/must/buy verbs.
